title: 1.4 Linked Lists
summary: ListNode class, traversal pattern, pointer manipulation, in-place reverse, node removal. Spaced-rep quiz at session open.
sessionNotes: Concepts clicked fast (4th time learning). Pointer manipulation took work but got all exercises passing. Needs practice: dry-running before tests, drawing diagrams on paper. Re-grounding session 2026-05-13 after a 27-day gap.
intro: Linked list = chain of nodes, each holding a value + pointer to the next. No contiguous memory, no index math — to reach node k, walk k pointers from head. Trade: O(1) insert/delete given a pointer, but O(k) access. Universal traversal pattern: `current = head; while current is not None: ...; current = current.next`. For reverse: three pointers (prev, curr, next_node) — save next_node before overwriting curr.next.

body.heading: What is a linked list?
body.content:
A linked list is a sequence of values, just like a Python list — but stored completely differently in memory.

A Python list (which is really an ARRAY under the hood) is stored as one contiguous block of memory. If you have [10, 20, 30, 40], those four values sit next to each other in RAM. To get element #2, the computer does:
    address_of_start + (2 * size_of_element)
→ instant. O(1) random access. This is why arrays are fast.

A LINKED LIST is stored as scattered nodes. Each node holds:
  1. a value
  2. a pointer (reference) to the NEXT node

The nodes can live ANYWHERE in memory. They're connected only by these pointers, like a treasure hunt.

To reach the 3rd node, you MUST start at head and follow pointers one by one. There is no formula to jump to index k.
→ O(k) access. This is linked lists' biggest weakness.

body.heading: Why do linked lists exist if arrays are faster?
body.content:
Two superpowers:

1. O(1) insert/delete at the HEAD (or anywhere you already hold a pointer to). No shifting. No resizing. You just rewire two pointers. Compare: lst.insert(0, x) on a Python list is O(n) because every element has to shift right by one.

2. No resize cost, no contiguous-memory requirement. A linked list can grow forever, one node at a time, wherever the allocator finds room.

The tradeoff: you give up random access and you pay extra memory for the pointers.

body.heading: Singly vs doubly linked
body.content:
Singly linked: each node points only to NEXT. You can only walk forward. Simple, less memory.

Doubly linked: each node points to BOTH next AND prev. You can walk in either direction, and you can delete a node in O(1) given just a pointer to it (because you can reach its neighbors directly). More memory per node.

We'll start with singly linked. 95% of interview questions use singly linked lists.

body.heading: The Node class
body.content:
class ListNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, next=None):
        self.val = val
        self.next = next

# Building [10, 20, 30] by hand:
head = ListNode(10)
head.next = ListNode(20)
head.next.next = ListNode(30)

# Equivalent (right-to-left, tail-first):
head = ListNode(10, ListNode(20, ListNode(30)))

print(head.val, head.next.val, head.next.next.val)

body.heading: The universal traversal pattern
body.content:
Memorize this. You will write it in every single linked list problem:

    current = head
    while current is not None:
        # do something with current.val
        current = current.next

Read it out loud: "start at head; while we haven't fallen off the end, do work, then step forward." The `current = current.next` line is how you advance — there is no `for i in range(len(...))` because there's no len and no index.

Time: O(n). Space: O(1) — only one pointer variable.

body.heading: Complexity cheat sheet (singly linked)
body.content:
Access index k:        O(k)      walk from head
  Search for a value:    O(n)      walk until found
  Insert at head:        O(1)      rewire 2 pointers
  Insert at tail:        O(n)*     unless you track tail
  Insert after a node:   O(1)      given the pointer
  Delete head:           O(1)
  Delete arbitrary node: O(n)      have to find prev first

  * O(1) if you maintain a tail pointer alongside head.

body.heading: Diagramming linked lists — an interview skill
body.content:
On a whiteboard or scratch paper, the standard approach:

1. DRAW EACH NODE as a box with two sections (val and next). The arrow coming out of "next" points to the next node. The last node's arrow points to a ground symbol or "None."

2. LABEL YOUR POINTER VARIABLES above or below the nodes with arrows pointing down at the node they reference.

3. STEP THROUGH YOUR LOOP by redrawing the pointer labels each iteration. Cross out old positions.

4. KEEP IT SMALL. Draw only 3–4 nodes. If your algorithm works on 3, it works on 300.

5. COMMON THINGS TO TRACK in your diagram: which node each variable points to RIGHT NOW, which .next pointers have been rewired, where None boundaries are.

body.heading: Example: 3-pointer reverse, iteration 1
body.content:
prev   curr   next_node
      ↓      ↓        ↓
     None  [1|•]──→[2|•]──→[3|None]

  After:  curr.next = prev    (1→None)
          prev = curr         (prev → [1])
          curr = next_node    (curr → [2])
          next_node = curr.next

exercises.title: Quiz Q1 — d.get(key, default)
exercises.prompt: What does d.get(key, default) do on a dict, and why is it useful for counting?
exercises.critique: Correct on the safety part. Missed the counting idiom (`d[k] = d.get(k, 0) + 1`) — that's the specific reason it's loved for frequency counters.

exercises.title: Quiz Q2 — .add() vs .append()
exercises.prompt: .add() vs .append() — which belongs to set, which to list?
exercises.critique: Correct. The why: sets are unordered, so there's no "end" to append to.

exercises.title: Quiz Q3 — duplicates one-liner
exercises.prompt: Write the one-liner that checks whether a list `nums` contains any duplicates, using a set.
exercises.critique: Inverted! `==` means lengths match → no duplicates → should return False. Should be `!=`. This was the classic boolean-inversion bug — leads to the "trace booleans with one example" habit being added to the queue.

exercises.title: Quiz Q4 — range(len(s)-1, -1, -1)
exercises.prompt: What does each of the three arguments mean? What sequence for s='abc'?
exercises.critique: Partial. The `-1` in start is NOT the indexing-meaning — it's the integer `len(s)-1` (the last index). The stop=-1 means "go below 0", exclusive — so 0 is included. step=-1 means decrement by 1 each step. The "step 2" worry: step is the increment between values, not a skip count — step=2 emits every other value going up, step=-1 emits each value going down. This item resets repeatedly.

exercises.title: Quiz Q5 — Two Sum dict design
exercises.prompt: Dict for Two Sum: (a) key? (b) value? (c) why that way?
exercises.critique: Correct on (a) and (b). For (c): more precisely — the key is what you LOOK UP (you ask "have I seen target - x?"), so the key must be the value being checked. The dict stores positions; the lookup is by value. Reversing it (index → value) would be useless because you don't know the index of what you need.

exercises.title: Quiz Q6 — amortized O(1)
exercises.prompt: What does 'amortized O(1)' mean for hash table insertion? Why isn't it just plain O(1)?
exercises.critique: Got the averaging intuition. More precise: most inserts ARE O(1) (the cheap case). Occasionally an insert triggers an O(n) resize (rare expensive case). Spread the resize cost across the n inserts that triggered it → O(1) per insert on average. The two-direction phrasing ("one more efficient, one less") isn't quite right — most inserts are exactly O(1), the rare ones are O(n).

exercises.title: Comprehension Check 1 — Why is array O(1) but linked list O(n) for indexing?
exercises.prompt: Why is indexing into a linked list O(n) while indexing into an array is O(1)? What's the physical/memory reason?
exercises.critique: Excellent. Captures both directions: array gets address arithmetic (`start + i × size`), linked list has no formula because nodes can be anywhere in memory — only pointers connect them.

exercises.title: Comprehension Check 2 — a.next.next.val
exercises.prompt: Given a→b→c, what does a.next.next.val evaluate to? Walk through it.
exercises.critique: Correct walkthrough. Each `.next` advances one node; `.val` reads the data field. This is just chained pointer following.

exercises.title: Comprehension Check 3 — why `while current is not None`?
exercises.prompt: Why does the traversal condition say `while current is not None` instead of `while current.next is not None`? Which nodes would you miss?
exercises.critique: Got the skip-last-node failure. Also worth noting: on an empty list (head=None), `current.next` would crash with AttributeError because None has no .next. So the broken version has TWO failure modes.

exercises.title: Exercise 1 — list_length
exercises.prompt: Given the head of a singly linked list, return how many nodes it contains. 0 for empty (head=None).
exercises.critique: Works. Style nit: `is not None` (identity check) is the canonical form, not `!= None` (equality check that can be overridden by custom classes). Variable `x` is fine but `count` would read better. Otherwise this is textbook traversal.

exercises.title: Exercise 2 — find_value
exercises.prompt: Return True if any node contains target, else False. Earliest exit point matters.
exercises.critique: Clean. Uses `is not None` this time — good. Early return as soon as match found. Returns False if loop finishes without a match. Standard search pattern.

exercises.title: Exercise 3 — reverse_list (canonical 3-pointer)
exercises.prompt: Reverse a singly linked list in place, return new head.
exercises.critique: Works correctly. Variable naming: `a/b/c` instead of the canonical `prev/curr/next_node` makes it harder to read at a glance. The standard form loops on `curr` and recomputes `next_node` inside, returning `prev` — your variant primes the loop with a pre-iteration setup (`a.next = None`) which works but is less common. Re-doing this with the canonical pattern is planned for the Thursday after consolidation.

exercises.title: Exercise 4 — remove_value
exercises.prompt: Remove the first node containing target. Return the (possibly new) head.
exercises.critique: Correct on the head-removal case (separate branch) and middle/tail removal (rewire prev.next). The `current.next = None` after rewiring is cosmetic — once nothing points to that node, it's garbage-collected; explicitly nulling its next isn't required. Style: the dual pointer `pre` + `current` is standard; the early-return-then-loop structure handles head separately. Compact two-pointer alternative: walk one pointer with `if curr.next.val == target`-style lookahead. Both work.

reviewItemsAdded: `is not None` vs `!= None` — use `is` for singletons
Linked list traversal: `while current is not None` pattern
Linked list: removing a node requires access to prev node
Trace booleans with one example before moving on
